title: Universal Theory of Incoherent Metals
authors: Aaron Kleger, Nikolay Gnezdilov, Rufus Boyack
abstract: Numerous unconventional superconductors such as cuprates, heavy-fermions, and twisted-bilayer graphene exhibit incoherent metallic transport above the superconducting critical temperature. This phenomenon cannot be described with Fermi-liquid theory and has presented a significant theoretical challenge to overcome. We utilize the two-dimensional Yukawa-SYK model of fermions with spatially random coupling to quantum-critical bosons to study transport in a manner which is non-perturbative in the coupling strength. Our work provides a microscopic model of quantum-critical incoherent metals and their concomitant properties, including a non-Boltzmann transport formula between resistivity and quasi-particle lifetime, violation of the Mott-Ioffe-Regel resistivity bound, and violation of the Kovtun-Son-Starinets shear viscosity to entropy density bound.
summary: This paper provides a microscopic theoretical framework for understanding 'bad metals' like cuprates and twisted bilayer graphene. By using a Yukawa-SYK model, the authors show how transport can break fundamental limits like the Mott-Ioffe-Regel bound. The work establishes a unified description of the crossover between strange-metal, bad-metal, and superconducting phases.
main_problem: Understanding the transport and thermodynamic properties of incoherent 'bad metals' where Fermi-liquid theory fails and standard transport bounds are violated.
main_result: The study establishes a microscopic model that demonstrates violations of the Mott-IOLoffe-Regel and KSS bounds, identifies a non-Boltzmann transport relation, and shows bosonic self-tuning to a quantum critical point.
method: A non-perturbative approach using the large-N limit of a (2+1)D Yukawa-SYK model, employing saddle-point equations and the Kubo formula for transport coefficients.
model_or_system: A (2+1)-dimensional Yukawa-Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (Y-SYK) model consisting of N electrons coupled to N bosons with spatially random coupling constants.
key_observables: Resistivity, electrical conductivity, shear viscosity to entropy density ratio (eta/s), charge diffusivity, and superconducting critical temperature (Tc).
important_parameters: Electronic bandwidth (Lambda), temperature (T), coupling strength (lambda), bosonic UV cutoff (omega_D), and impurity scattering rate (Omega_0).
main_assumptions: Large-N limit, momentum-independent self-energies due to local disorder, and use of natural units (hbar = kB = 1).
figures_summary: Plots of resistivity versus temperature for various coupling strengths, showing the transition from strange-metal to bad-metal regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Y-SYK model, derives self-consistent saddle-point equations, analyzes transport regimes (Strange Metal, Bad Metal, Thermal Incoherent Metal), investigates the impact of disorder and self-tuning, and concludes with the derivation of superconducting properties.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Phase diagram for the (2+1)d Y-SYK model with spatially random electron-boson coupling tuned to the QCP. The KSS bound is violated above the dotted black line.

FIG. 2. Renormalized boson mass as a function of tempera- ture T/Ω0 in the impurity regime for M0 = ωD. The inset shows the renormalized boson masses in (0+1)d and (2+1)d at low T/Ω0, where θ = λΛ/(3π)2.
FIG. 3. (a) Resistivity (in units of h/e2) as a function of temperature T/εF .

FIG. 4. Shear viscosity to entropy density ratio η/s (in units of ℏ/(4πkB)) for T > 0.01εF . Subplot shows η/s in the weak- coupling regime. Dashed blue line denotes the KSS bound: ℏ/(4πkB). The values of λ are given in the legend of Fig. 3.

FIG. 5. Shear viscosity to entropy density ratio η/s (in units of ℏ/(4πkB)) as a function of dimensionless coupling, λ, eval- uated at T = 0.05εF . Red and blue curves denote η/s for the infinite and finite bandwidth limit solutions respectively. Inset displays η/s on log-log scales. Dashed blue and gray lines denotes the KSS bound: ℏ/(4πkB) and asymptotic val- ues respectively.
